title: National Register of Historic Places listings in Kandiyohi County, Minnesota
rows: Farmstead of John Bosch ( 1898-1978 ) , a founder and leader of the Farmers ' Holiday Association , the nation 's most radical and successful agricultural protest movement of the Great Depression . Comprises seven contributing properties built 1885-1936
Andreas , Johanna , Anna and Frank E. Broman Farmstead
Off County Road 8 between Swan Lake and Kasota Lake 45°04′35″N 94°54′34″W / 45.076506°N 94.909499°W / 45.076506 ; -94.909499 ( Andreas , Johanna , Anna and Frank E. Broman Farmstead )

Unusually intact example of west-central Minnesota 's late-19th-century farmsteads , with structures dating to 1885 ; occupied by three generations of a family representative of the region 's Swedish American populace
1907 school significant as one of the key meeting sites where the Farmers ' Holiday Association was organized in the early 1930s . Also known as the Svea School
Off County Highway 5 45°11′02″N 95°06′22″W / 45.183863°N 95.106129°W / 45.183863 ; -95.106129 ( Lars and Guri Endreson House )

Circa-1858 log cabin , one of Kandiyohi County 's oldest buildings on its original site and a rare vestige of its Euro-American settlement prior to the Dakota War of 1862 , in which numerous pioneers were killed ( including two Endresons ) and most of the rest abandoned the area for several years . Now a museum
Prominent and rare surviving example of west-central Minnesota 's railroad-era downtown hotels , built in 1904 to attract commerce
County Rd . 134 ; east shore of Kasota Lake 45°04′38″N 94°52′51″W / 45.077315°N 94.880848°W / 45.077315 ; -94.880848 ( Kasota Lake Site )
Pre-contact domestic site with well-stratified ceramic artifacts , indicating 2,700 years of use across the Woodland , Plains Village , and Oneota periods
1927 hotel that provided Willmar 's finest accommodations to traveling salesmen and tourists and a popular social venue to locals
1876 commercial building expanded circa 1885 , noted for its well-preserved Italianate architecture and association with the early development of Willmar as a railroad town
Sibley State Park CCC/Rustic Style Historic District
Off U.S. Route 71 west of New London 45°18′49″N 95°02′13″W / 45.313729°N 95.036995°W / 45.313729 ; -95.036995 ( Sibley State Park CCC/Rustic Style Historic District )

19 park facilities built 1935-38 , significant as highly intact examples of New Deal federal work relief , early state park development , National Park Service rustic design , and successful master planning
Albert H. and Jennie C. Sperry House
228 Porto Rico St. 45°07′35″N 95°02′15″W / 45.1263°N 95.0376°W / 45.1263 ; -95.0376 ( Albert H. and Jennie C. Sperry House )
John M. Spicer Summer House and Farm
Well-preserved early-20th-century summer home complex in a popular resort area . Also associated with influential land developer John M. Spicer ( 1841-1928 ) . Now the Spicer Castle Inn & Restaurant
Municipal auditorium built 1935-38 as a major work relief project . Also noted for its fine Moderne architecture and interior artwork
Willmar Hospital Farm for Inebriates Historic District
Off U.S. Route 71 45°08′32″N 95°01′06″W / 45.142147°N 95.018295°W / 45.142147 ; -95.018295 ( Willmar Hospital Farm for Inebriates Historic District )

Minnesota 's most intact early state hospital complex , with 22 contributing properties built 1912-1933 , exemplifying the era 's novel cottage-based design and the work of Clarence H. Johnston Sr. Now the MinnWest Technology Campus

Airport established in 1934 as a federal work relief project , with a 1941 hangar built by the National Youth Administration . Also associated with prominent longtime manager John L. Rice ( serving 1945-1983 )

Newspaper office active 1920-1980 , associated with the regionally influential Willmar Daily Tribune and its longtime owner-turned-politician Victor E. Lawson ( 1871-1960 ) , a prominent advocate for populist reforms

description: This table lists properties in Kandiyohi County, Minnesota, that are included in the National Register of Historic Places. It provides details such as the name of the property, the date it was listed, its location, the associated city or town, and a brief description of its historical significance. The table serves as a resource for understanding the cultural and historical heritage of the region.
